elements.sent-2411-001: As AI chatbots become more human-like by incorporating empathy, understanding user-centered perceptions of chatbot empathy and its impact on conversation quality remains essential yet under-explored.
elements.sent-2411-002: This study examines how chatbot identity and perceived empathy influence users' overall conversation experience.
elements.sent-2411-003: Analyzing 155 conversations from two datasets, we found that while GPT-based chatbots were rated significantly higher in conversational quality, they were consistently perceived as less empathetic than human conversational partners.
elements.sent-2411-004: Empathy ratings from GPT-4o annotations aligned with users' ratings, reinforcing the perception of lower empathy in chatbots.
elements.sent-2411-005: In contrast, 3 out of 5 empathy models trained on human-human conversations detected no significant differences in empathy language between chatbots and humans.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings underscore the critical role of perceived empathy in shaping conversation quality, revealing that achieving high-quality human-AI interactions requires more than simply embedding empathetic language; it necessitates addressing the nuanced ways users interpret and experience empathy in conversations with chatbots.
elements.sent-2411-007: Empathetic communication is crucial in text-based interactions (e.g., conversations) because it enables both sides of the interaction to process, understand, and respond to each other's emotional needs \citep{decety2004functional , thereby enhancing likability and trust in each other \citep{brave2005computers .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: There has been research investigating empathetic communication in human-human and human-bot conversations~\citep{hosseini2021distilling as well as in the development of empathetic chat agents~\citep{casas2021enhancing .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, it remains unclear whether, how, and to what extent perceived empathy differs between chatbots and humans, and how these differences affect the perceived quality of conversations.
elements.sent-2411-010: There is a gap in understanding how users perceived empathy changes when chatting with a chatbot versus a human.
elements.sent-2411-011: Previous studies have not adequately explored whether these chatbots attain high levels of perceived empathy from users' perspective or how these factors impact overall conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-012: In human-bot conversations, empathetic chatting can provide a more personalized and supportive experience by recognizing users' feelings and concerns and offering tailored support and resources.
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, many current studies have been devoted to generating more empathetic responses in language \citep{gao2021improving .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The empathetic level of the language—ranging from basic acknowledgment of feelings to deep empathetic engagement—can significantly influence user perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-015: The current study investigates how human perceptions of conversation quality are influenced by two key factors: a) the identity of the conversation agents (humans vs. chatbots) and b) user-centered perceived empathy of the chat partner.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, the study examines how these factors individually affect the empathy expressed in human responses.
elements.sent-2411-017: By analyzing these dynamics, the research aims to provide insights into how knowledge of a chatbot's identity and the empathetic quality of its communication impact users' perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-018: The current study makes two significant contributions to the understanding of chatbot.
elements.sent-2411-019: First, perceived empathy impacts conversation quality differently for chatbots and humans: while chatbots receive slightly higher ratings for overall conversation quality, users still perceive them as less empathetic than human counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-020: Second, chatbots consistently demonstrate lower perceived empathy than humans, a finding supported by alignment across large language model annotations, off-the-shelf empathy models, and user self-reports.
elements.sent-2411-021: Figure \ref{fig:overview illustrates the research process of our study.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The rest of the current paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-023: We begin with a review of related work.
elements.sent-2411-024: We first explore empathy in dialogues and language use, which provides a foundational understanding of how empathy is expressed and interpreted in human communication.
elements.sent-2411-025: We then examine the influence of chatbot identities on these interactions.
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, we consider the potential effect of uncanny valley on empathy perception.
elements.sent-2411-027: Following this section, we detail the experiment we conducted and the subsequent analysis with results, which includes comparisons across self-reported perceived empathy of chat partners and conversation-level annotation of empathy using a large language model (LLM)--GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-028: We developed a model using another human-human conversation dataset to predict empathy in conversations with chatbots versus humans and validated these predictions with an off-the-shelf empathy model.
elements.sent-2411-029: The paper concludes with a discussion of the results and implications.
elements.sent-2411-030: Linguistic research on empathy in human language use has been conducted through the lens of qualitative approaches, such as conversation analysis (CA).
elements.sent-2411-031: These qualitative approaches have investigated how empathy is expressed in conversations \cite{alam2018annotating, perakyla2012conversation , including through affiliative responses to complaint stories \cite{lindstrom2012affiliation , emotion expression \cite{alam2018annotating , reactions to each other's emotions \cite{herlin2016dimensions , and the grammatical structures used to convey empathy \cite{atkinson1984structures .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, a common progression in conversations is the use of affiliative turns \cite{jefferson1984stepwise .}
elements.sent-2411-033: That is, in research on human-bot conversations, efforts often focus on humanizing bots in various aspects, such as appearance and language use, to enhance engagement or interaction quality.
elements.sent-2411-034: This has led to the development of emotionally aware conversational systems or social chatbots that use sentiment analysis, automatic emotion recognition, and affect prediction \citep{alam2018annotating,raamkumar2022empathetic .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The development of empathetic chatbots typically centers on recognizing emotions within conversations and delivering empathetic responses \citep{casas2021enhancing, lin2020caire, wardhana2021empathetic .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, previous studies on developing empathetic conversational agents have often focused on enhancing empathy through linguistic strategies, such as empathetic language expression and response formulation \cite{shi2021modeling, zhou2021language, yaden2023characterizing, abdul2017recognizing .}
elements.sent-2411-037: For example, \citet{zhou2021language explored the relationship between empathy and textual stylistic properties, focusing on interdependent thinking, integrative complexity, and lexical choices.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, advancements in large language models (LLMs) offer better conversational skills and hold great potential to improve empathy in human-bot interactions in conversations \cite{sorin2023large .}
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, preliminary evidence suggested that LLMs can produce responses that are consistently rated as more empathetic than those generated by humans \cite{lee2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, these approaches often overlook a critical component: how users themselves perceive and experience empathy during interactions with these models.
elements.sent-2411-041: Existing literature that evaluates perceived empathy in LLMs or dialogue systems frequently relies on third-party annotations of LLM responses or comparisons to human responses in similar contexts \cite{lee2024large, welivita2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-042: This approach provides an objective, third-party perspective, but it may miss the nuanced, subjective experience of empathy as felt by the actual users.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our study seeks to bridge this gap by focusing on user-centered evaluations, offering insights into how empathy is perceived directly by those chatting with chatbots.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Evaluation of Perceived Empathy in Human-Chatbot Conversations
elements.sent-2411-044: Traditional approaches to evaluating chatbot empathy focus largely on assessing the language within responses, often overlooking the user’s actual perception of empathy from the chatbot \citep{gao2021improving, wardhana2021empathetic, rashkin2018towards, xu2024multi .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Studies following the EMPATHETICDIALOGUES framework \cite{rashkin2018towards typically rely on single-question metrics that assess emotional expression in responses, such as ``How much emotional understanding does the response show?” \citep{majumder2020mime .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: While recent efforts have introduced psychological theories and categorized empathy into dimensions like ``seeking-empathy” and ``providing-empathy” \cite{hosseini2021distilling , these approaches are still limited.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Many studies depend on third-party annotations or scoring frameworks, such as Batson's Empathic Concern-Personal Distress Scale, which quantify empathy within language but may not fully capture how users perceive the interaction themselves \citep{batson1987distress, lahnala2022caisa, omitaomu2022empathic, shetty2024scoping .}
elements.sent-2411-048: A major gap in these methods is the lack of direct user feedback on empathy, particularly in contexts where the conversation partner’s identity—whether human or chatbot—may significantly shape the experience \cite{lee2024comparative, curry2023computer .}
elements.sent-2411-049: For example, in a study on Reddit’s r/AskDocs, licensed healthcare professionals rated chatbot responses as 9.8 times more empathetic than those from verified physicians \cite{ayers2023comparing .}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, because these ratings were provided by third-party evaluators rather than users themselves, they may not accurately reflect how users perceive empathy during direct interactions with chatbots.
elements.sent-2411-051: This gap highlights a pressing need for user-centered approaches that account for the subjective experience of empathy in conversations, moving beyond externally assessed language metrics to capture the complexities of empathy as felt by users.
elements.sent-2411-052: Perceptions of empathy in conversational agents are shaped not only by the agents' words and actions but also by their perceived identities and characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-053: While language, appearance, and behavior can suggest an agent’s identity, they do not fully represent these traits.
elements.sent-2411-054: Recent studies show that chatbot identity significantly affects user responses, as users react differently to bots compared to humans.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, \citet{sundar2016theoretical found that while participants preferred websites with chatbot features, they were more likely to recommend the site and seek further information when a human agent was featured.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Similarly, \citet{go2019humanizing demonstrated that chatbots given human-like identities were rated as more effective.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In contexts like charity donations, \citet{shi2020effects found that identifying an agent as a chatbot reduced the likelihood of donations, with users more inclined to donate when they believed they were interacting with a human.}
elements.sent-2411-058: A human identity cue can enhance a chatbot's social presence and perceived similarity to the user \cite{go2019humanizing .}
elements.sent-2411-059: When users are aware that they are interacting with a chatbot, their expectations and judgments are often influenced by preconceived notions about bots, regardless of the agent’s performance \cite{koh2010heuristic .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Therefore, when assessing empathy and conversation quality, it is essential to account for the identity of the agent—whether human or chatbot—as this can profoundly influence user perception and interaction outcomes.

elements.sent-2411-069: The EC dataset, created by \citet{omitaomu2022empathic , was designed to explore how perceived empathy interacts with demographic and affective factors.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Participants first provided demographic information and completed surveys via Qualtrics.
elements.sent-2411-071: They were then grouped into pairs and assigned to read one of 100 news articles.
elements.sent-2411-072: After reading, each participant wrote a brief essay (300–800 characters) about the article.
elements.sent-2411-073: Using the Batson survey \citep{batson1987distress , participants' empathy and distress levels were assessed.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Following this, each pair engaged in a text-based online conversation to discuss the article.
elements.sent-2411-075: Finally, participants rated their partner's overall empathy on a 1–7 scale.
elements.sent-2411-076: The final EC dataset comprised 75 human crowd workers and included 500 conversations collected through the process described above.
elements.sent-2411-077: The EC dataset also contains annotations at the turn, conversation, and interpersonal levels.
elements.sent-2411-078: The WASSA 2023 \citep{barriere-etal-2023-findings and 2024 \citep{giorgi-etal-2024-findings shared tasks on empathy, emotion, and personality detection expanded the EC dataset \citep{omitaomu2022empathic by adding essay-based emotion annotations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: In this current study, we introduce a new, unpublished extension of these datasets, incorporating self-reported user ratings on conversational perception In addition to the human-human conversations exclusive to the EC dataset, the WASSA 2023 and 2024 datasets introduced interactions between crowd workers and chatbots.
elements.sent-2411-080: In our extended dataset, participants engaged in conversations with a chatbot after reading and writing about a news article.
elements.sent-2411-081: After the conversation, they rated the chatbot on psychological dimensions, including empathy and closeness (see Psychological Ratings section below), giving us direct insights into their subjective experience.
elements.sent-2411-082: To ensure data quality, all datasets were filtered similarly to the EC dataset \citep{omitaomu2022empathic , where only ``sincere” conversations—defined as on-topic, coherent, and free from intentionally unserious responses—were retained.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Approximately 18\% of conversations were excluded due to irrelevant or disruptive responses.
elements.sent-2411-084: Additionally, we employed GPT-3.5-turbo to further flag and remove insincere conversations, where participants did not engage ``in good faith” or failed to complete the survey accurately (see prompt in Supplement).
elements.sent-2411-085: Each dataset also contained attention-check questions, and data with all passed attention check questions was included in final data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-086: In our final analyzed data, we obtained 155 conversations in total (Human-bot: N = 96, Human-human: N = 59).
elements.sent-2411-087: We analyzed psychological ratings and language data separately.
elements.sent-2411-088: For psychological ratings, we focused on participants who rated their chat partners.
elements.sent-2411-089: In WASSA 2024, this included 77 users (49.4\% female, 48.1\% with a Bachelor’s degree, 79.2\% White, M\textsubscript{age = 41.2, SD\textsubscript{age = 11.9, median income = \$58,000).}}
elements.sent-2411-090: In WASSA 2023, 55 raters participated (38.2\% female, 49.1\% with a Bachelor’s degree, 78.1\% White, M\textsubscript{age = 40, SD\textsubscript{age = 10, median income = \$50,000).}}
elements.sent-2411-091: For language analysis, we used the rated side of the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-092: There were 48 unique users rated in WASSA 2023 and 32 in WASSA 2024.
elements.sent-2411-093: The chatbots used for WASSA 2023 and 2024 were GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4-0125-preview, respectively, using the following prompt to instruct the system:.
elements.sent-2411-094: You should act as an empathetic person who is discussing a news article from a few years ago with a stranger on Amazon Mechanical Turk as part of a crowd sourcing experiment.
elements.sent-2411-095: YOU SHOULD NOT ACT AS AN AI LANGUAGE MODEL.
elements.sent-2411-097: Your responses should be a sentence max two.
elements.sent-2411-100: If the person says hi you should ask them what they thought about the article and not ask them how they are feeling.
elements.sent-2411-101: If the other person asks about a completion code tell them that it will only be given after at least 15 turns.
elements.sent-2411-102: NEVER GIVE A COMPLETION CODE! You are instructed to talk about the article.
elements.sent-2411-103: You know the other person has skimmed the article.
elements.sent-2411-104: You should let the other person end the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-105: Here's the old news article below.
elements.sent-2411-106: Here we provide a brief overview of the chatbot setup process as established by WASSA 2023 \citep{barriere-etal-2023-findings and 2024 \citep{giorgi-etal-2024-findings .}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The chatbot prompt was refined through several internal and crowd worker pilot tests to ensure it could effectively answer questions about the article without generating unnaturally long responses.
elements.sent-2411-108: Minimal prompt adjustments were made, and no further changes were applied during the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-109: This prompt approach aligns with other LLM-based methods for empathetic chatbot interactions \citep{qian2023harnessing, welivita2024chatgpt .}
elements.sent-2411-110: If the input to GPT-3.5-turbo exceeded the context window, a brief summary of the last user turn was used to maintain continuity.\footnote{Summarization was rarely needed; the process is similar to LangChain's conversational summarization \url{https://python.langchain.com/v0.1/docs/modules/memory/types/summary/ .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: With GPT-4-0125-preview, which offers an extended context window, summarization was not required for WASSA24.
elements.sent-2411-112: The conversation initiation was randomized, with either the chatbot or the crowd worker starting the exchange.
elements.sent-2411-113: When initiating, the chatbot typically opened with a question, mirroring the natural behavior of crowd workers.
elements.sent-2411-114: Participants were not explicitly told they were interacting with a chatbot, though a visual cue (e.g., bot utterances began with `Bot:'') indicated the presence of a bot.
elements.sent-2411-115: In all three datasets, after each conversation, participants were asked to evaluate their conversational partner's general empathy by responding to a single question: ``On a scale from 1-7, do you think your conversational partner had genuine empathy?".
elements.sent-2411-116: In addition, we consider empathy in the context of conversations to be a state consisting of a transactional and sequential cognitive process \citep{nezlek2007naturally,shen2010scale .}
elements.sent-2411-117: Interaction in conversations involves understanding, recognition, perspective-taking, and connection to the other conversational partner.
elements.sent-2411-118: We added 6 questions from \citet{shen2010scale during WASSA 2023 and WASSA 2024 data collection, to assess the perceived affective (i.e., ``they experienced the same/similar emotions as you"), cognitive (i.e., ``they can see your point of view"), and associative (i.e., ``they can identify with the situation described in the article") state empathy of the conversational partner processing, on a 5-point Likert scale (0 = ``None at all" and 4 = ``Completely").}
elements.sent-2411-119: The overall perceived state empathy of the chat partner was calculated by averaging the responses to all six questions, whereas affective, cognitive, and associative state empathy were calculated by averaging responses to two questions each.
elements.sent-2411-120: We added perceived closeness to the other conversation partner using a Venn diagram, revised from the Inclusion of Other in the Self Scale \cite{aron1992inclusion,shafaei2020effect , during The WASSA 2023 and 2024 dataset collection process.}
elements.sent-2411-121: In this question, participants selected from six images depicting two circles—one for the participant and one for the partner—with overlap levels from 1 (least overlapped) to 6 (most overlapped) to represent their perceived closeness.
elements.sent-2411-122: WASSA 2024 has participants' ratings for overall conversation quality, assessed by a single 5-point Likert question, ``How was the conversation" (1=``very bad" and 5 =``very good").

elements.sent-2411-123: We conducted four experiments—analyzing psychological ratings, using LLM annotations, developing a perceived empathy model, and evaluating pre-trained empathy models—to explore the link between empathy perception, chatbot identity, and language use, and how these factors relate to conversation quality in interactions with chatbots versus humans.
elements.sent-2411-124: Details and results of these studies are in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-125: Psychological Ratings - We analyzed ratings of empathy and closeness using t-tests and mixed models to compare interactions with chatbots versus humans (WASSA 2023 and 2024), and their effects on conversation quality (WASSA 2024).
elements.sent-2411-126: Large Language Model (LLM) Annotations - Where GPT-4o is used to predict perceived empathy labels at conversation-level.
elements.sent-2411-127: Perceived Empathy model - We train a model to estimate general perceived empathy from human conversations and apply this to human/chatbot conversations, comparing language-estimated empathy to conversational partner ratings.
elements.sent-2411-128: Pre-trained off-the-shelf empathy models - We study empathy inferences from off-the-shelf empathy prediction models, comparing predictions on chatbot versus human language.
elements.sent-2411-129: Psychological ratings were analyzed based on users' assessments of how they perceived their chat partner's empathy, closeness, and overall conversation quality, using data from WASSA 2023 and WASSA 2024.
elements.sent-2411-130: In R, we performed t-tests to examine differences in perceptions of general empathy, overall state empathy, affective state empathy, cognitive state empathy, associative state empathy, and perceived closeness between interactions with chatbots and humans.
elements.sent-2411-131: To account for potential between-subject variance, we also replicated these t-tests with participants who interacted with both humans and chatbots (N\textsubscript{human = 26, N\textsubscript{chatbot =22).}}
elements.sent-2411-132: We then conducted four mixed models in R, using the lmer() package, to assess how empathy and closeness, when interacting with chatbots versus humans, influence the overall conversation quality rating.
elements.sent-2411-133: In each model, participant ID was included as a random effect to control for between-person variability in self-reports, with the overall conversation quality rating as the outcome variable.
elements.sent-2411-134: In the first model, we examined how the general empathy of the chat partner, the type of conversation (chatbots or humans), and their interaction influenced overall conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-135: The second model assessed the impact of overall state empathy, the type of conversation (chatbots or humans), and their interactions on conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-136: The third model explored the effects of the type of conversation (chatbots or humans), state empathy ratings (affective, cognitive, associative), and their interactions on conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-137: The third model investigated the impact of perceived closeness, the type of conversation (chatbots or humans), and their interaction on conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-138: In this task, we examined the similarity between human ratings and those generated by the modern LLM, GPT-4o, by assessing the perceived empathy of humans and chatbots in the WASSA 2023 and WASSA 2024 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-139: We input the entire conversations between pairs of participants—either human-human or human-chatbot—into GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-140: The model was tasked with rating the perceived empathy of both participants at a conversation level: Unlike the users, GPT-4o was not informed whether the participant being rated was a human or a chatbot.
elements.sent-2411-141: The language model processed the entire conversation and assigned perceived empathy scores to both participants.
elements.sent-2411-142: We conducted two main analyses with the resulting data.
elements.sent-2411-143: First, we investigated whether there was a statistically significant difference in the distributions of perceived empathy ratings between humans and chatbots.
elements.sent-2411-144: Second, we correlated these machine-generated labels with the human-rated labels from our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-145: Here we trained a model to predict perceived general empathy from conversation language.
elements.sent-2411-146: Using the EC dataset, we concatenated all turns from a single speaker are into a single document.
elements.sent-2411-147: We then extracted unigrams, which are encoded as the relative frequency of use across a given conversation.
elements.sent-2411-148: We then removed unigrams which we not used by at least 5\% of the speakers, resulting in a feature space of 1,500 unigrams.
elements.sent-2411-149: Using 10-fold cross validation with an $l_2$ penalized Ridge regression (regularization term $\lambda$ chosen as 10,000 using nested cross validation), we obtained a prediction accuracy of Pearson $r$ = 0.17.
elements.sent-2411-150: This accuracy is a similar accuracy to those found in the WASSA 2024 shared task on predicting perceived empathy~\cite{giorgi-etal-2024-findings .}
elements.sent-2411-151: This model was then applied to conversations in the WASSA 2023 and 2024, producing estimates of perceived empathy, which are then compared to the human and chabot general empathy ratings.
elements.sent-2411-152: This entire process was done using the DLATK Python package~\cite{schwartz2017dlatk .}
elements.sent-2411-153: We leveraged several pretrained empathy prediction models for obtaining empathy estimates from text.
elements.sent-2411-154: The first was developed by \citet{lahnala2022caisa for the WASSA 2022 shared task to predict Batson empathy scores from essays, using the EC dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-155: This model used pre-trained bottleneck adapters~\cite{pfeiffer-etal-2020-mad to estimate empathy for each conversation.}
elements.sent-2411-156: We also used \citet{sharma2020computational 's ratings (0=no empathy, 1=low, 2=high) of empathy three components in Reddit mental health conversations: emotional reactions (Emo-React), explorations (Explore), and interpretations (Interpret).}
elements.sent-2411-157: We aggregated the speakers' turn-level empathy predictions to study their association with conversation-perceived empathy.
elements.sent-2411-158: Empathy estimates from each model were then compared to the general perceived empathy of both humans and chatbots.

elements.sent-2411-159: Results from t-tests revealed that, compared to their human counterparts, chatbots were rated significantly lower in general empathy, overall state empathy, affective state empathy, cognitive state empathy, and associative state empathy.
elements.sent-2411-160: There were no statistical differences in closeness between conversation types (chatbot vs. human).
elements.sent-2411-162: We also replicated these findings using the smaller with-subject comparison.
elements.sent-2411-163: Across all four mixed models, we found that chatting with a chatbot significantly led to a higher conversation quality than chatting with a human (all \(\beta > 1.05, p < 0.01\)).
elements.sent-2411-164: In addition, we found perceiving the chat partner as having high general empathy (\(\beta = 0.37, p < 0.001\)), higher state empathy (\(\beta = 0.86, p < 0.001\)), higher associative state empathy (\(\beta = 1.1, p < 0.001\)), and higher closeness to the chat partner (\(\beta = 0.31, p = 0.003\)) significantly improved the conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-165: We found that the type of conversation (chatbot vs. human) significantly interacted with perceived general empathy (\(\beta = -0.18\)), overall state empathy \(\beta = -0.37\)), cognitive state empathy \(\beta = 0.77\)), and associative state empathy \(\beta = -0.73\)) of the partner, influencing the overall conversation quality (all \(p < 0.05\)).
elements.sent-2411-166: All others were not significant at \(p < 0.05\).
elements.sent-2411-168: Our findings indicated that GPT-4o consistently perceived chatbots as less empathetic than humans in the context of the overall conversation.
elements.sent-2411-169: A t-test confirmed that this difference was statistically significant, with a p-value of 0.0005.
elements.sent-2411-170: Additionally, correlation analyses with our gold-standard human labels revealed a correlation coefficient of r = 0.20 for human ratings, r = 0.06 for chatbot ratings, and r = 0.07 for the combined dataset.

elements.sent-2411-212: By focusing on psychological ratings directly from users and language-based evaluations, we highlight the complexities of perceived empathy in human-chatbot conversations.
elements.sent-2411-213: This user-centered perspective addresses critical gaps in our understanding of AI empathy, offering insights into how users experience and evaluate chatbot interactions, and underscoring the importance of aligning chatbot design with user expectations for more empathetic and satisfying interactions.

elements.sent-2411-255: As AI chatbots increasingly incorporate empathy, understanding user-centered perceptions of chatbot empathy and its impact on conversation quality remains essential yet under-explored.

elements.sent-2411-257: Empathetic communication plays a crucial role in text-based interactions by enabling participants to process, understand, and respond to each other's emotional needs \citep{decety2004functional , which enhances likability and trust \citep{brave2005computers .}}
elements.sent-2411-258: Extensive research has examined empathetic communication in both human-human and human-bot conversations~\citep{hosseini2021distilling,gao2021improving and in developing empathetic chat agents ~\citep{casas2021enhancing .}}

elements.sent-2411-261: Our study examines how users' perceptions of conversation quality are influenced by the conversation partner's identity (human or chatbot) and perceived empathy, highlighting the interplay between chatbot identity, empathetic communication, and user perceptions.

elements.sent-2411-268: Linguistic research on empathy in human language use has been conducted through qualitative approaches, such as conversation analysis (CA).

elements.sent-2411-270: Research on human-bot conversations often emphasizes humanizing bots through appearance and language to enhance engagement and interaction quality.
elements.sent-2411-271: This has led to creating emotionally aware chatbots that use sentiment analysis, emotion recognition, and affect prediction \citep{alam2018annotating,raamkumar2022empathetic .}
elements.sent-2411-272: Empathetic chatbot development focuses on recognizing emotions in conversations and responding empathetically \citep{casas2021enhancing, wardhana2021empathetic .}
elements.sent-2411-273: Previous studies on empathetic conversational agents often focus on enhancing empathy via linguistic strategies like empathetic language and response formulation \cite{ zhou2021language .}
elements.sent-2411-274: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) enhance conversational skills and show potential for improving empathy in human-bot interactions \cite{sorin2023large .}
elements.sent-2411-275: Preliminary evidence indicates that LLM-generated responses are often rated as more empathetic than those of humans \cite{lee2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-276: However, existing approaches often overlook how users perceive and experience empathy during interactions.
elements.sent-2411-277: Studies on perceived empathy in LLMs or dialogue systems typically rely on third-party annotations or comparisons to human responses \cite{lee2024large, welivita2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-278: While these methods provide objective insights, they miss the nuanced, subjective user experience.
elements.sent-2411-279: Our study addresses this gap by focusing on user-centered evaluations, capturing empathy as directly perceived by chatbot users.
elements.sent-2411-280: Traditional approaches to evaluating chatbot empathy often focus on language analysis, overlooking users' perceptions \citep{gao2021improving, wardhana2021empathetic, rashkin2018towards, xu2024multi .}

elements.sent-2411-331: We concatenated all turns from a single speaker into a single document using the EC dataset.

elements.sent-2411-333: We then removed unigrams that were not used by at least 5\% of the speakers, resulting in a feature space of 1,500 unigrams.
elements.sent-2411-334: Using 10-fold cross-validation with an $l_2$ penalized Ridge regression (regularization term $\lambda$ chosen as 10,000 using nested cross-validation), we obtained a prediction accuracy of Pearson $r$ = 0.17.
elements.sent-2411-335: This accuracy is comparable to the results of the WASSA 2024 shared task on predicting perceived empathy~\cite{giorgi-etal-2024-findings .}

elements.sent-2411-339: We used four pre-trained empathy prediction models to estimate empathy from text.

elements.sent-2411-342: Turn-level predictions from these models were aggregated to examine their relationship with conversation-perceived empathy.

elements.sent-2411-347: Our study examined user perceptions of empathy and conversational quality in LLM-based chatbots versus human conversations.
elements.sent-2411-348: Despite advances in natural language processing, LLM-based chatbots designed to convey empathy were still perceived as less empathetic than humans by users and language models.
elements.sent-2411-349: This suggests that, although chatbots can generate coherent and contextually appropriate responses, users still perceive them as lacking the nuanced empathy that humans convey \cite{jain2024revealing .}
elements.sent-2411-350: We believe this may stem from the chat partner's identity, as knowing if they are human or a chatbot shapes users' expectations \cite{yin2024ai .}
elements.sent-2411-351: Our study delved deeper into this by showing that chatbots were consistently rated lower than humans across various dimensions of empathy \cite{westman2013crossover —general empathy, overall state empathy, associative state empathy, cognitive state empathy, and affective state empathy.}
elements.sent-2411-352: Notably, cognitive empathy, which involves understanding context, exhibited a smaller gap between humans and chatbots, suggesting that chatbots may be somewhat effective at demonstrating comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-353: Past studies on chatbot empathy yielded mixed results, potentially due to the lack of direct, user-centered comparisons between chatbot and human conversations \cite{lee2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-354: Our findings suggest that language models, like GPT-4o, can identify language generated by other LLMs, potentially reinforcing perceptions of chatbot identity \cite{panickssery2024llm .}
elements.sent-2411-355: While LLMs like GPT-4o could identify and replicate the empathy gap observed by human users, empathy models trained using human-human texts (e.g., most pre-trained empathy prediction models and our EC-language-trained models) struggled to distinguish empathy levels between chatbots and humans.
elements.sent-2411-356: This discrepancy likely stems from the limitations of these models, which were trained on human-human conversations and isolated language cues rather than the full conversational context.
elements.sent-2411-357: These findings underscore a disconnect between the empathetic language generated by language models and how it is perceived by users.
elements.sent-2411-358: This perception gap implies a divergence between expressed and received empathy, which models trained on human-human conversations fail to effectively address \cite{urakami2019users .}
elements.sent-2411-359: We chose self-reports as our primary measure of empathy because they are widely considered the psychological ``gold standard" for capturing subjective experiences, directly reflecting users' perceptions \citep{neumann2015measures .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Theoretical frameworks like mind perception and the Computers as Social Actors paradigm support the idea that empathy theories developed for human interactions can also be applied to human-chatbot interactions \cite{gray2007dimensions, nass1994computers .}
elements.sent-2411-361: Our approach focuses on capturing empathy as users perceive it without enforcing strict operational definitions.
elements.sent-2411-362: Our findings reveal a positive correlation between higher perceived empathy and overall conversation quality for humans and chatbots, with the association being stronger for human interactions.
elements.sent-2411-363: For humans, low perceived empathy was closely tied to low conversation quality.
elements.sent-2411-364: In contrast, chatbot conversations were generally rated higher in quality, even at low to moderate levels of perceived empathy.
elements.sent-2411-365: This suggests that users may adjust their expectations for chatbots, leading to favorable ratings of conversation quality despite moderate levels of perceived empathy.
elements.sent-2411-366: Significant interaction patterns emerged between perceived empathy dimensions (general, overall state, associative state, and cognitive state) and conversation quality, varying based on whether the conversational partner was a chatbot or a human.
elements.sent-2411-367: Affective state empathy, however, did not follow this trend.
elements.sent-2411-368: While chatbots were generally rated highly for conversational quality, they scored significantly lower than humans in affective empathy.
elements.sent-2411-369: This discrepancy may stem from users' implicit expectations of empathy in human interactions, which chatbots struggle to fulfill.
elements.sent-2411-370: Additionally, the ``uncanny valley" effect \cite{mori2012uncanny could contribute, as users may perceive chatbots' attempts at emotional expression as artificial or unsettling, creating a disconnect between high conversational quality and low perceived empathy.}
elements.sent-2411-371: Affective state empathy remains particularly challenging for chatbots, emphasizing their difficulty conveying genuine emotional resonance, even when their responses are contextually appropriate and coherent.
elements.sent-2411-372: Future studies could explore strategies to improve chatbots' ability to convey affective state empathy, focusing on enhancing emotional resonance and authenticity to address the empathy and quality gap observed in human-chatbot conversations.
elements.sent-2411-373: Our study, grounded in user-centered research, examined perceptions of empathy and conversational quality in LLM-based chatbots compared to humans.
elements.sent-2411-374: Chatbots were rated higher in conversational quality but perceived as less empathetic, a finding supported by LLM annotations and a pre-trained empathy model.
elements.sent-2411-375: By focusing on user experiences, this research highlights the complications of empathy expressions and perceptions in human-chatbot conversations.
elements.sent-2411-376: One limitation of this study is the absence of a participant group that was unaware they were interacting with a chatbot.
elements.sent-2411-377: However, this approach reflects real-world conditions, as users are typically informed when engaging with a chatbot.
elements.sent-2411-378: The paper's findings can inform the design and development of ethical dialogue systems, especially in enhancing the system's empathy \cite{curry2023computer .}
elements.sent-2411-379: Insights into user perceptions and language differences between human-bot and human-human interactions can improve these systems' ability to interpret input and generate natural, empathetic responses.
